properties.name: Rosa Parks Museum
properties.description: Located at 252 Montgomery Street, the museum marks the site where Rosa Parks was arrested for refusing to give up her bus seat, sparking the 381-day Montgomery Bus Boycott.

properties.name: Dexter Avenue King Memorial Baptist Church
properties.description: At 454 Dexter Avenue, this National Historic Landmark is where Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. served as pastor (1954-1960) and helped organize the Montgomery Bus Boycott.

properties.name: The Legacy Museum
properties.description: At 400 N. Court Street, this EJI museum tells the story of racial injustice from slavery through mass incarceration using interactive media and first-person narratives.

properties.name: National Memorial for Peace and Justice
properties.description: At 417 Caroline Street, the nation's first memorial dedicated to victims of lynching, featuring over 800 steel monuments representing counties where racial terror lynchings occurred.

properties.name: First Baptist Church (Brick-a-Day)
properties.description: At 347 N. Ripley Street, this historic church sheltered Freedom Riders in May 1961 when a mob surrounded the building. Dr. King addressed the gathering by phone from the besieged church.

properties.name: Freedom Rides Museum
properties.description: At 210 S. Court Street, the restored 1951 Greyhound Bus Station is where Freedom Riders were attacked by a segregationist mob upon arrival in Montgomery on May 20, 1961.

properties.name: Alabama State Capitol
properties.description: On Dexter Avenue, the Capitol was the destination of the 54-mile Selma to Montgomery marches in March 1965, where Dr. King delivered his 'How Long, Not Long' speech to 25,000 marchers.

properties.name: Brown Chapel AME Church
properties.event: Selma to Montgomery March Starting Point
properties.description: In Selma, Alabama, Brown Chapel AME Church served as the starting point and organizational headquarters for the three Selma to Montgomery voting rights marches in 1965.

properties.name: Selma to Montgomery National Historic Trail Marker (Mile 54)
properties.description: Near the Alabama State Capitol, this marker commemorates the final mile of the 54-mile Selma to Montgomery march along US Route 80, designated a National Historic Trail in 1996.

properties.name: MLK National Historic Trail Marker
properties.description: Along Dexter Avenue in downtown Montgomery, this marker is part of the Martin Luther King Jr. National Historic Trail connecting key sites of the civil rights movement.
